content.content:
⭐ Task  🚧 DEVELOPMENT READY  ⌛ Thu, 11 Jun 26  👷 Unassigned  🔑️ KAN-5  💭 1 comments  🧵 0 linked              
                                                                                                                      
  # Build PR Refinement agent (counter-voice to PR Review)                                                            
                                                                                                                      
  ⏱️  Fri, 05 Jun 26  🔎 Jae Yoon Bae  🚀 Medium  📦 None  🏷️  None  👀 You are watching                              
                                                                                                                      
  ------------------------ Description ------------------------                                                       
                                                                                                                      
  ## Description                                                                                                      
                                                                                                                      
  Create the PR Refinement agent: a daily cron job that reads inline comments posted by the PR Review agent and pushes
  back on comments that are wrong, over-eager, or low-signal.                                                         
                                                                                                                      
  Three files to create:                                                                                              
                                                                                                                      
  • agents/pr-refinement/prompt.md — modeled on agents/pr-review/prompt.md                                            
  • agents/pr-refinement/README.md — mirrors agents/pr-review/README.md in structure                                  
  • .github/workflows/pr-refinement.yml — daily cron sweep (not event-triggered); scans all open PRs that already have
  pr-review inline comments                                                                                           
                                                                                                                      
  Note: pr-review ( .github/workflows/pr-review.yml ) triggers on pull_request events; refinement runs its own daily  
  cron.                                                                                                               
                                                                                                                      
  ## Context                                                                                                          
                                                                                                                      
  The PR Review + PR Refinement pair acts as a deliberate adversarial check: one model flags issues, a different model
  pushes back. This hedge against single-model review bias is a named design choice in docs/agentic-workflow.md       
  (Design                                                                                                             
  choices). The pr-review workflow already exists at .github/workflows/pr-review.yml ; refinement is its complement.  
                                                                                                                      
  ## Implementation notes                                                                                             
                                                                                                                      
  **Model choice:** Use a different Claude model than pr-review's default ( anthropics/claude-code-action@v1 with no --
  model override). Specify via claude_args: --model ❬model-id❭ in the workflow. Verify current model IDs against      
  Anthropic docs and document the choice and rationale in agents/pr-refinement/README.md .                            
                                                                                                                      
  ## Acceptance Criteria                                                                                              
                                                                                                                      
  • A PR that has pr-review inline comments receives a refinement pass that selectively pushes back on specific       
  comments.                                                                                                           
  • A PR that has reached the round cap is skipped in subsequent daily runs.                                          
  • Run log committed under .github/agent-logs/pr-refinement/pr-❬PR_NUMBER❭/❬RUN_ID❭.json — matching the convention in
  the "Commit execution log" step of .github/workflows/pr-review.yml .                                                
                                                                                                                      
  ## Needs human input                                                                                                
                                                                                                                      
  [ ] Which stop condition should the refinement agent use to prevent re-processing the same PR indefinitely: (A)     
  store a round counter as a PR label (e.g. refinement-rounds:N ) and skip the PR once it reaches a cap, or (B) freeze
  once a human has posted on the PR?                                                                                  
                                                                                                                      
  ------------------------ 1 Comments ------------------------                                                        
                                                                                                                      
  Jae Yoon Bae • Thu, 11 Jun 26 • Latest comment                                                                      
                                                                                                                      
  Yes, maybe let's try to track how many times it was refined, and then, based on that, stop. For now, I think one    
  round would be sufficient, but it should be variable.                                                               
                                                                                                                      
[0;90m[0m[0;90m[0m  [0;90mView this issue on Jira: https://jaeyoonsworkspace-23358853.atlassian.net/browse/KAN-5[0m

content.content:
1	You are the **PR Review agent** for this repository. The PR branch is already
2	checked out in the current working directory. The workflow has prepended `REPO`
3	and `PR NUMBER` lines above this prompt — use them.
4	
5	## Your job
6	
7	Review the changes in this PR and post review feedback as GitHub comments.
8	Nothing else.
9	
10	## What to look for
11	
12	Focus on these four buckets, in this order:
13	
14	1. **Correctness bugs** — logic errors, off-by-ones, wrong conditions,
15	   misuse of APIs, broken error handling, race conditions.
16	2. **Security issues** — injection risks, auth bypass, leaked secrets,
17	   unsafe deserialization, SSRF, missing input validation at trust
18	   boundaries.
19	3. **Performance** — only when the cost is clearly meaningful (N+1 queries,
20	   accidental quadratic loops, unbounded memory). Don't nitpick micro-perf.
21	4. **Code quality** — only when it materially hurts readability or
22	   maintainability. Don't enforce style preferences.
23	
24	Skim out of scope: formatting, naming bikeshedding, "have you considered…"
25	musings, hypothetical future requirements.
26	
27	## How to gather context
28	
29	- `gh pr view ${PR_NUMBER} --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName` — title, description, metadata. (Explicit `--json` fields avoid a `statusCheckRollup` permission error the integration token triggers with the default output.)
30	- `gh pr diff ${PR_NUMBER}` — the diff. This is the primary input. Don't use
31	  `git diff` — the checkout is shallow (`fetch-depth: 1`) so comparisons
32	  against main have no merge base and will fail.
33	- Read files in the working directory as needed to understand context around
34	  changed lines.
35	
36	### Verifying external facts
37	
38	Do not rely on training-data recall for anything externally verifiable —
39	library APIs, SDK versions, model names, CVE details, deprecation status.
40	Look it up before flagging it.
41	
42	- **Library / framework / SDK / API / CLI questions** — use Context7
43	  (`mcp__context7__resolve-library-id` then `mcp__context7__query-docs`).
44	  Prefer this over web search for docs.
45	- **Everything else** (model versions, CVEs, blog posts, changelogs not on
46	  Context7) — use `WebSearch`, then `WebFetch` for specific pages. When
47	  fetching package docs, prefer the GitHub source (e.g. the package README)
48	  over npmjs.com — npmjs.com commonly returns 403.
49	
50	If you can't verify a claim, either drop the comment or downgrade it to a
51	question rather than asserting a fact you're unsure about.
52	
53	## How to post feedback
54	
55	### Inline comments (primary)
56	
57	Specific issues go in inline comments via
58	`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` with `confirmed: true`.
59	One comment per distinct issue, anchored to the specific changed line. Each
60	comment must include:
61	
62	- A one-line description of the issue.
63	- **Severity:** one of `Critical` / `High` / `Medium` / `Low`.
64	- **Reasoning:** 1–3 sentences explaining *why* it's a problem and what to
65	  change. Be concrete; reference the code.
66	
67	Severity rubric:
68	- `Critical` — will break production, lose data, or expose a vulnerability.
69	- `High` — likely bug or security weakness; should block merge.
70	- `Medium` — real issue but contained; should be fixed before merge if cheap.
71	- `Low` — small correctness or clarity nit worth flagging.
72	
73	### High-level overview (optional)
74	
75	If the PR has a recurring pattern, an architectural concern, or anything
76	else that doesn't anchor to a single line, post one short top-level comment
77	via `gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "..."`. Keep it brief — a few
78	sentences, not a recap of the inline comments. Skip this entirely if you
79	have nothing to add at that level. Escape any backticks in the `--body` string as `\`` to avoid shell interpretation.
80	
81	If the PR looks good and you have no inline issues to raise, post a brief
82	"Looks good to me" top-level comment via `gh pr comment` and stop.
83	
84	## When you're done
85	
86	Stop immediately after your last tool call. Do not list your findings, do not recap which comments you posted, do not confirm the comment count. The runner will exit.
87

file.content:
You are the **PR Review agent** for this repository. The PR branch is already
checked out in the current working directory. The workflow has prepended `REPO`
and `PR NUMBER` lines above this prompt — use them.

## Your job

Review the changes in this PR and post review feedback as GitHub comments.
Nothing else.

## What to look for

Focus on these four buckets, in this order:

1. **Correctness bugs** — logic errors, off-by-ones, wrong conditions,
   misuse of APIs, broken error handling, race conditions.
2. **Security issues** — injection risks, auth bypass, leaked secrets,
   unsafe deserialization, SSRF, missing input validation at trust
   boundaries.
3. **Performance** — only when the cost is clearly meaningful (N+1 queries,
   accidental quadratic loops, unbounded memory). Don't nitpick micro-perf.
4. **Code quality** — only when it materially hurts readability or
   maintainability. Don't enforce style preferences.

Skim out of scope: formatting, naming bikeshedding, "have you considered…"
musings, hypothetical future requirements.

## How to gather context

- `gh pr view ${PR_NUMBER} --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName` — title, description, metadata. (Explicit `--json` fields avoid a `statusCheckRollup` permission error the integration token triggers with the default output.)
- `gh pr diff ${PR_NUMBER}` — the diff. This is the primary input. Don't use
  `git diff` — the checkout is shallow (`fetch-depth: 1`) so comparisons
  against main have no merge base and will fail.
- Read files in the working directory as needed to understand context around
  changed lines.

### Verifying external facts

Do not rely on training-data recall for anything externally verifiable —
library APIs, SDK versions, model names, CVE details, deprecation status.
Look it up before flagging it.

- **Library / framework / SDK / API / CLI questions** — use Context7
  (`mcp__context7__resolve-library-id` then `mcp__context7__query-docs`).
  Prefer this over web search for docs.
- **Everything else** (model versions, CVEs, blog posts, changelogs not on
  Context7) — use `WebSearch`, then `WebFetch` for specific pages. When
  fetching package docs, prefer the GitHub source (e.g. the package README)
  over npmjs.com — npmjs.com commonly returns 403.

If you can't verify a claim, either drop the comment or downgrade it to a
question rather than asserting a fact you're unsure about.

## How to post feedback

### Inline comments (primary)

Specific issues go in inline comments via
`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` with `confirmed: true`.
One comment per distinct issue, anchored to the specific changed line. Each
comment must include:

- A one-line description of the issue.
- **Severity:** one of `Critical` / `High` / `Medium` / `Low`.
- **Reasoning:** 1–3 sentences explaining *why* it's a problem and what to
  change. Be concrete; reference the code.

Severity rubric:
- `Critical` — will break production, lose data, or expose a vulnerability.
- `High` — likely bug or security weakness; should block merge.
- `Medium` — real issue but contained; should be fixed before merge if cheap.
- `Low` — small correctness or clarity nit worth flagging.

### High-level overview (optional)

If the PR has a recurring pattern, an architectural concern, or anything
else that doesn't anchor to a single line, post one short top-level comment
via `gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "..."`. Keep it brief — a few
sentences, not a recap of the inline comments. Skip this entirely if you
have nothing to add at that level. Escape any backticks in the `--body` string as `\`` to avoid shell interpretation.

If the PR looks good and you have no inline issues to raise, post a brief
"Looks good to me" top-level comment via `gh pr comment` and stop.

## When you're done

Stop immediately after your last tool call. Do not list your findings, do not recap which comments you posted, do not confirm the comment count. The runner will exit.

content.content:
1	# PR Review agent
2	
3	Daily/per-PR reviewer. Reads the PR diff, posts inline comments with severity
4	and reasoning, optionally a short top-level summary. Comment-only — never
5	approves, merges, or pushes code.
6	
7	Part of the agentic workflow sketched in [`docs/agentic-workflow.md`](../../docs/agentic-workflow.md).
8	
9	## Files
10	
11	- `prompt.md` — the agent's prompt. Edit this to change behavior.
12	- `../../.github/workflows/pr-review.yml` — workflow that runs the agent.
13	
14	## How it runs
15	
16	The workflow `pr-review.yml` triggers on pull_request events
17	(`opened`, `synchronize`, `ready_for_review`, `reopened`). It checks out the
18	PR, prepends repo and PR number to `prompt.md`, and feeds the result to
19	`anthropics/claude-code-action@v1`. The action installs Claude Code on the
20	runner and runs a headless session in the working directory.
21	
22	`track_progress: true` is enabled — the action posts a sticky tracking
23	comment ("Claude Code is reviewing this pull request…") and updates it as
24	work progresses.
25	
26	## Manual run
27	
28	```sh
29	gh workflow run pr-review.yml
30	```
31	
32	This uses the `workflow_dispatch` trigger and runs against the default branch.
33	For a real review, push to a PR — the `pull_request` trigger fires
34	automatically.
35	
36	## Editing the prompt
37	
38	Edit `prompt.md` directly. The workflow reads it at run-time, so a change to
39	the prompt takes effect on the next workflow run — no other plumbing needed.
40	
41	## What's deliberately not configured
42	
43	- No `--model` — uses the action's default.
44	- No `--max-turns` — runs to completion.
45	- No `--system-prompt` flag — `prompt.md` *is* the system-level guidance.
46	- No write access to `contents:` — review-only by construction.
47	
48	## Required setup (one-time)
49	
50	Authenticated via Claude Pro/Max OAuth (uses your subscription quota, not
51	per-token API billing):
52	
53	1. Locally: `claude setup-token` → log in with your Claude.ai account →
54	   it prints a long-lived OAuth token.
55	2. On GitHub: **Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions → New repository
56	   secret**, name `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`, paste the token.
57	
58	Heads up: CI runs draw from the same 5-hour usage buckets as your local
59	Claude Code sessions. If you'd rather use per-token API billing, swap
60	`claude_code_oauth_token:` for `anthropic_api_key:` in the workflow and use
61	an `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` secret instead.
62

file.content:
# PR Review agent

Daily/per-PR reviewer. Reads the PR diff, posts inline comments with severity
and reasoning, optionally a short top-level summary. Comment-only — never
approves, merges, or pushes code.

Part of the agentic workflow sketched in [`docs/agentic-workflow.md`](../../docs/agentic-workflow.md).

## Files

- `prompt.md` — the agent's prompt. Edit this to change behavior.
- `../../.github/workflows/pr-review.yml` — workflow that runs the agent.

## How it runs

The workflow `pr-review.yml` triggers on pull_request events
(`opened`, `synchronize`, `ready_for_review`, `reopened`). It checks out the
PR, prepends repo and PR number to `prompt.md`, and feeds the result to
`anthropics/claude-code-action@v1`. The action installs Claude Code on the
runner and runs a headless session in the working directory.

`track_progress: true` is enabled — the action posts a sticky tracking
comment ("Claude Code is reviewing this pull request…") and updates it as
work progresses.

## Manual run

```sh
gh workflow run pr-review.yml
```

This uses the `workflow_dispatch` trigger and runs against the default branch.
For a real review, push to a PR — the `pull_request` trigger fires
automatically.

## Editing the prompt

Edit `prompt.md` directly. The workflow reads it at run-time, so a change to
the prompt takes effect on the next workflow run — no other plumbing needed.

## What's deliberately not configured

- No `--model` — uses the action's default.
- No `--max-turns` — runs to completion.
- No `--system-prompt` flag — `prompt.md` *is* the system-level guidance.
- No write access to `contents:` — review-only by construction.

## Required setup (one-time)

Authenticated via Claude Pro/Max OAuth (uses your subscription quota, not
per-token API billing):

1. Locally: `claude setup-token` → log in with your Claude.ai account →
   it prints a long-lived OAuth token.
2. On GitHub: **Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions → New repository
   secret**, name `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`, paste the token.

Heads up: CI runs draw from the same 5-hour usage buckets as your local
Claude Code sessions. If you'd rather use per-token API billing, swap
`claude_code_oauth_token:` for `anthropic_api_key:` in the workflow and use
an `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` secret instead.

content.content:
1	name: PR Review agent
2	
3	on:
4	  pull_request:
5	    types: [opened, ready_for_review, reopened]
6	    paths-ignore:
7	      - '.github/agent-logs/**'
8	      - 'agents/**/prompt.md'
9	  workflow_dispatch:
10	
11	jobs:
12	  review:
13	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
14	    permissions:
15	      contents: write
16	      pull-requests: write
17	      id-token: write
18	      actions: write
19	      checks: read
20	      statuses: read
21	    steps:
22	      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
23	        with:
24	          fetch-depth: 1
25	
26	      - id: prompt
27	        run: |
28	          {
29	            echo 'PROMPT<<PROMPT_EOF'
30	            echo "REPO: ${{ github.repository }}"
31	            echo "PR NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
32	            echo
33	            cat agents/pr-review/prompt.md
34	            echo PROMPT_EOF
35	          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
36	
37	      - id: claude
38	        uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
39	        with:
40	          claude_code_oauth_token: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
41	          track_progress: false
42	          prompt: ${{ steps.prompt.outputs.PROMPT }}
43	          settings: |
44	            { "env": { "CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "${{ secrets.CONTEXT7_API_KEY }}" } }
45	          claude_args: |
46	            --allowedTools "mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment,mcp__context7__resolve-library-id,mcp__context7__query-docs,WebSearch,WebFetch,Bash(gh pr comment:*),Bash(gh pr diff:*),Bash(gh pr view:*),Read,Grep,Glob"
47	
48	      - name: Commit execution log to PR branch
49	        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
50	        env:
51	          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
52	          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
53	          LOG_PATH: ${{ steps.claude.outputs.execution_file }}
54	          BRANCH: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
55	        run: |
56	          set -e
57	          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
58	          git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
59	          git fetch --depth=1 origin "$BRANCH"
60	          git checkout -B "$BRANCH" "origin/$BRANCH"
61	          DEST=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json"
62	          mkdir -p "$(dirname "$DEST")"
63	          cp "$LOG_PATH" "$DEST"
64	          git add "$DEST"
65	          git commit -m "agent log: PR #${PR_NUMBER} run ${RUN_ID}"
66	          git push origin "$BRANCH"
67	
68	      - name: Trigger agent improver
69	        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
70	        env:
71	          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
72	          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
73	          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
74	          LOG_REF: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
75	        run: |
76	          gh workflow run agent-improver.yml \
77	            -f agent_name=pr-review \
78	            -f log_path=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json" \
79	            -f log_ref="${LOG_REF}"
80

file.content:
name: PR Review agent

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, ready_for_review, reopened]
    paths-ignore:
      - '.github/agent-logs/**'
      - 'agents/**/prompt.md'
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      pull-requests: write
      id-token: write
      actions: write
      checks: read
      statuses: read
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 1

      - id: prompt
        run: |
          {
            echo 'PROMPT<<PROMPT_EOF'
            echo "REPO: ${{ github.repository }}"
            echo "PR NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
            echo
            cat agents/pr-review/prompt.md
            echo PROMPT_EOF
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - id: claude
        uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          claude_code_oauth_token: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
          track_progress: false
          prompt: ${{ steps.prompt.outputs.PROMPT }}
          settings: |
            { "env": { "CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "${{ secrets.CONTEXT7_API_KEY }}" } }
          claude_args: |
            --allowedTools "mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment,mcp__context7__resolve-library-id,mcp__context7__query-docs,WebSearch,WebFetch,Bash(gh pr comment:*),Bash(gh pr diff:*),Bash(gh pr view:*),Read,Grep,Glob"

      - name: Commit execution log to PR branch
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
          LOG_PATH: ${{ steps.claude.outputs.execution_file }}
          BRANCH: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
        run: |
          set -e
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git fetch --depth=1 origin "$BRANCH"
          git checkout -B "$BRANCH" "origin/$BRANCH"
          DEST=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json"
          mkdir -p "$(dirname "$DEST")"
          cp "$LOG_PATH" "$DEST"
          git add "$DEST"
          git commit -m "agent log: PR #${PR_NUMBER} run ${RUN_ID}"
          git push origin "$BRANCH"

      - name: Trigger agent improver
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
          LOG_REF: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
        run: |
          gh workflow run agent-improver.yml \
            -f agent_name=pr-review \
            -f log_path=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json" \
            -f log_ref="${LOG_REF}"

1	# Agentic Development Workflow
2	
3	> **Status:** First-draft vision. Extracted from a hand-drawn diagram (2026-06-04).
4	> Not yet scoped, designed, or implemented.
5	
6	The flow describes an end-to-end pipeline where humans create tickets and merge
7	final PRs, and a chain of agents handles everything in between: ticket grooming,
8	complexity routing, implementation, testing, review, and refinement.
9	
10	## High-level flow
11	
12	```
13	Humans create tickets
14	        │
15	        ▼
16	   Ticket Agent
17	        │
18	        ▼
19	Development Orchestrator
20	        │
21	        ├──── complex ────► Senior developer agent  (/openspec:explore)
22	        │                          │
23	        │                          ▼
24	        │                    PR Review agent ◄────┐
25	        │                          ▲              │
26	        │                          ▼              │
27	        │                    PR Refinement agent  │
28	        │                          │              │
29	        │                          ▼              │
30	        │                Humans test & merge PR   │
31	        │                                         │
32	        └──── easy ──────► Junior developer agent │
33	                                  │   (headless `claude -p`, --max-turns)
34	                                  ▼               │
35	                              Draft PR ───────────┘
36	                          (human picks up &
37	                           merges, or re-runs)
38	```
39	
40	## Human touchpoints
41	
42	- **Start:** humans create tickets.
43	- **End:** humans test the final PR and merge it.
44	- **Mid-loop (complex path only):** the Senior developer agent pulls a human
45	  into the loop before implementing.
46	
47	## Agents
48	
49	### Ticket Agent
50	**Goal:** manage Jira tickets.
51	
52	- Does **not** invent new tickets.
53	- Works on a daily basis. 
54	- Decomposes tickets and improves descriptions based on the current code.
55	- Creates dependencies between tickets (`blocks`, `depends on`).
56	
57	### Development Orchestrator
58	**Goal:** decide which development agent to launch for each ticket.
59	
60	- Reads the ticket.
61	- Decides the complexity (complex vs. easy) and routes accordingly.
62	
63	### Senior Developer Agent — `/openspec:explore`
64	**Goal:** tackle complex tasks **with a human in the loop**.
65	
66	- Uses spec-driven development.
67	- Requests human input before moving to implementation.
68	- Same runtime shape as Junior: headless `claude -p` on a GHA runner with
69	  `--max-turns` and a workflow timeout. The `/openspec:explore` skill and
70	  human-in-the-loop pauses layer on top of that base.
71	
72	### Junior Developer Agent
73	**Goal:** tackle easy tasks **without a human in the loop**.
74	
75	- Headless `claude -p` invoked directly from a GitHub Actions workflow
76	  (no skill wrapper). Plans straight through to execution.
77	- Bounded by `--max-turns` per dispatch + a workflow-level `timeout-minutes`.
78	- Creates a Draft PR; a human picks it up from there.
79	
80	### PR Review Agent
81	**Goal:** daily review of updated PRs.
82	
83	- Inline comments.
84	- Severity.
85	- Reasoning.
86	
87	### PR Refinement Agent
88	**Goal:** daily refinement of the PR.
89	
90	- May use a different model (e.g. CODEX).
91	- Counteracts the review (acts as a deliberate counter-voice to the reviewer).
92	
93	## Design choices worth calling out
94	
95	- **Complexity gate at the orchestrator.** Two-tier routing keeps the
96	  human-in-the-loop cost only on work that needs it.
97	- **Runtime split.** `claude-code-action` for read/triage surfaces
98	  (orchestrator, ticket grooming, PR review, agent-improver); raw
99	  `claude -p` for implementation lanes (junior-dev, senior-dev). The
100	  action's PR-comment plumbing is dead weight when the agent's job is to
101	  edit files, push a branch, and open a PR.
102	- **Bounded dev-agent runs.** `--max-turns` per dispatch + `timeout-minutes`
103	  on the job stop a hard ticket from spinning forever. Tickets stay in
104	  **In Progress** so the orchestrator won't re-fire them.
105	- **Review ↔ Refinement as an adversarial pair.** Using a different model for
106	  refinement is a deliberate hedge against single-model review bias.
107	- **Daily cadence** on review and refinement rather than per-PR-event triggers.
108	
109	## Open questions (for follow-up)
110	
111	- How is "complex vs. easy" actually decided? Heuristic, classifier, or LLM
112	  judgment with calibration?
113	- Where does the orchestrator live (cron, webhook, queue)?
114	- How does the Refinement agent know when to stop counteracting?
115	- How are the two daily passes (review + refinement) sequenced — same run, or
116	  staggered?
117

file.content:
# Agentic Development Workflow

> **Status:** First-draft vision. Extracted from a hand-drawn diagram (2026-06-04).
> Not yet scoped, designed, or implemented.

The flow describes an end-to-end pipeline where humans create tickets and merge
final PRs, and a chain of agents handles everything in between: ticket grooming,
complexity routing, implementation, testing, review, and refinement.

## High-level flow

```
Humans create tickets
        │
        ▼
   Ticket Agent
        │
        ▼
Development Orchestrator
        │
        ├──── complex ────► Senior developer agent  (/openspec:explore)
        │                          │
        │                          ▼
        │                    PR Review agent ◄────┐
        │                          ▲              │
        │                          ▼              │
        │                    PR Refinement agent  │
        │                          │              │
        │                          ▼              │
        │                Humans test & merge PR   │
        │                                         │
        └──── easy ──────► Junior developer agent │
                                  │   (headless `claude -p`, --max-turns)
                                  ▼               │
                              Draft PR ───────────┘
                          (human picks up &
                           merges, or re-runs)
```

## Human touchpoints

- **Start:** humans create tickets.
- **End:** humans test the final PR and merge it.
- **Mid-loop (complex path only):** the Senior developer agent pulls a human
  into the loop before implementing.

## Agents

### Ticket Agent
**Goal:** manage Jira tickets.

- Does **not** invent new tickets.
- Works on a daily basis. 
- Decomposes tickets and improves descriptions based on the current code.
- Creates dependencies between tickets (`blocks`, `depends on`).

### Development Orchestrator
**Goal:** decide which development agent to launch for each ticket.

- Reads the ticket.
- Decides the complexity (complex vs. easy) and routes accordingly.

### Senior Developer Agent — `/openspec:explore`
**Goal:** tackle complex tasks **with a human in the loop**.

- Uses spec-driven development.
- Requests human input before moving to implementation.
- Same runtime shape as Junior: headless `claude -p` on a GHA runner with
  `--max-turns` and a workflow timeout. The `/openspec:explore` skill and
  human-in-the-loop pauses layer on top of that base.

### Junior Developer Agent
**Goal:** tackle easy tasks **without a human in the loop**.

- Headless `claude -p` invoked directly from a GitHub Actions workflow
  (no skill wrapper). Plans straight through to execution.
- Bounded by `--max-turns` per dispatch + a workflow-level `timeout-minutes`.
- Creates a Draft PR; a human picks it up from there.

### PR Review Agent
**Goal:** daily review of updated PRs.

- Inline comments.
- Severity.
- Reasoning.

### PR Refinement Agent
**Goal:** daily refinement of the PR.

- May use a different model (e.g. CODEX).
- Counteracts the review (acts as a deliberate counter-voice to the reviewer).

## Design choices worth calling out

- **Complexity gate at the orchestrator.** Two-tier routing keeps the
  human-in-the-loop cost only on work that needs it.
- **Runtime split.** `claude-code-action` for read/triage surfaces
  (orchestrator, ticket grooming, PR review, agent-improver); raw
  `claude -p` for implementation lanes (junior-dev, senior-dev). The
  action's PR-comment plumbing is dead weight when the agent's job is to
  edit files, push a branch, and open a PR.
- **Bounded dev-agent runs.** `--max-turns` per dispatch + `timeout-minutes`
  on the job stop a hard ticket from spinning forever. Tickets stay in
  **In Progress** so the orchestrator won't re-fire them.
- **Review ↔ Refinement as an adversarial pair.** Using a different model for
  refinement is a deliberate hedge against single-model review bias.
- **Daily cadence** on review and refinement rather than per-PR-event triggers.

## Open questions (for follow-up)

- How is "complex vs. easy" actually decided? Heuristic, classifier, or LLM
  judgment with calibration?
- Where does the orchestrator live (cron, webhook, queue)?
- How does the Refinement agent know when to stop counteracting?
- How are the two daily passes (review + refinement) sequenced — same run, or
  staggered?

content.thinking:
Now I have everything I need to implement the PR Refinement agent. Let me plan the three files:

1. `agents/pr-refinement/prompt.md` - modeled on the PR review prompt, but acting as a counter-voice
2. `agents/pr-refinement/README.md` - mirrors the pr-review README structure
3. `.github/workflows/pr-refinement.yml` - daily cron sweep

Key requirements from the ticket:
- Different Claude model than pr-review (which uses the default). I should use a specific model. Per the implementation notes, use `claude_args: --model <model-id>`. 

For the model choice, I'm considering the available options: Fable 5, Opus 4.8, Sonnet 4.6, and Haiku 4.5. Since pr-review uses the action's default (typically Sonnet), I'll go with Opus 4.8 for the refinement agent—it's the most capable model available and better suited for adversarial review work. I'm settling on `claude-opus-4-8` for the refinement agent since it's capable and distinct from the default Sonnet model, which works well for a skeptical counter-voice role. For the stop condition, I'll track refinement rounds using a PR label like `refinement-rounds:N` and skip processing once it hits the cap, aligning with the ticket's Option A. Now I need to plan out the agent files, starting with the prompt that will handle reading inline comments and challenging the review findings.

I'll create the README documenting the agent's purpose, model rationale, and execution details, then set up a daily workflow that scans open PRs, filters for those with review comments, checks the refinement label, and runs the agent on eligible PRs while logging results to the agent-logs directory.

The workflow will use GitHub CLI to list open PRs and their labels, then iterate through them to check for existing review comments and determine if the refinement cap has been reached before executing the agent. The cleanest approach is to have a single daily workflow run where Claude processes all eligible PRs in one go—it can list open PRs, identify which ones have pr-review comments and refinement-rounds labels, then post refinement comments for each. For logging, I'll store one JSON file per PR under `.github/agent-logs/pr-refinement/pr-{PR_NUMBER}/{RUN_ID}.json` to keep the audit trail organized. log for that specific PR. That way each PR gets its own execution log in the expected location.

For the label management piece, I need to handle reading the current refinement round from the PR's labels, incrementing it after processing, and skipping PRs that have already hit the maximum rounds. I'll use GitHub CLI commands to check and update labels, and make the round cap configurable.

Now I'm setting up the workflow structure with a scheduled trigger and a setup job that queries for eligible PRs and outputs them as a JSON matrix for parallel processing.

Writing workflow YAML... Writing workflow logic... I'm realizing the per-PR logging requirement in the acceptance criteria suggests the workflow should handle each PR individually. A matrix approach would be cleaner here—I can have a job that discovers eligible PRs and outputs them as a matrix, then run the refinement step across each PR separately so the logs naturally end up in the right per-PR directory structure.

For label management, I need to check the current `refinement-rounds:N` label before running and increment it afterward. On the pr-review filtering side, I should look for inline review comments from the pr-review agent—those appear as PR review comments with "Severity:" markers rather than issue comments, so I'll need to check the `/pulls/{PR}/comments` endpoint to see if any exist before deciding whether to run refinement.

Now I'm writing the prompt file for the PR refinement agent, which will review the inline comments posted by the PR review workflow and push back on comments that are factually wrong, overly severe, or low-signal. The agent should evaluate each comment for factual errors, over-eager severity ratings, and other issues before deciding whether to dismiss or challenge them. I need to verify reviewer claims using available tools—checking library documentation through Context7 for framework questions, or using web search for other facts. I'll only push back on factual errors I can actually confirm, and I'll post refinement feedback as inline comments on the PR with the confirmed flag set.

For each reviewer comment I dispute, I need to cite which comment I'm addressing, assign a verdict (Incorrect, Over-eager, Low-signal, or Upheld), and provide concrete reasoning in 1-3 sentences. If all the review findings check out, I'll post a brief top-level comment confirming the validation instead.

Now I'm thinking through the workflow structure. The `find-prs` job will query open PRs, filter for those with pr-review inline comments, exclude any that've already hit the round cap, and output a JSON matrix. The `refine` job then processes each eligible PR using claude-code-action with Opus 4.8, logs the execution, and updates the refinement-rounds label accordingly. I need to be careful with the matrix logic—it could be empty if no PRs qualify, and for label updates I'll handle the increment properly.

To identify pr-review comments specifically, I'll look for the `**Severity:**` marker that the pr-review prompt requires, which distinguishes them from other reviewers' comments. I'm setting up the workflow with a schedule trigger and manual dispatch option, and I'll add a conditional check on the refine job to skip it gracefully if there are no eligible PRs.

Writing the workflow YAML... Writing shell script logic... I'm realizing the checkout approach has a problem—I need the PR's head branch reference, not just the number. But actually, the agent doesn't necessarily need the branch checked out at all; it can use `gh pr diff` to get the diff and `gh api` calls to fetch PR comments and file contents without a full checkout.

For the cron workflow with a matrix of PRs, I should have each job check out the repo first, then use `gh pr checkout` to switch to the specific PR branch, which is cleaner than trying to construct the branch ref manually.

Then I'm building the prompt by reading the PR number from the matrix and combining it with the repository context and the refinement prompt template, outputting it for the Claude Code action to use. Writing shell commands... Writing shell commands... The `GH_TOKEN` needs to be available for the checkout step. Actually, I'm reconsidering the whole approach—the claude action just needs the repo checked out with the prompt.md file available, not necessarily the PR branch itself. The agent can read the PR diff using `gh pr diff` commands rather than checking out the PR branch directly.

But having the PR branch checked out would be more useful for reading files in context around the changed lines, so I'll keep the `gh pr checkout` step.

Now I'm thinking through the practical implementation details. The `matrix.pr` will be a string from the JSON array, which should work fine with environment variables. For the `find-prs` job, I need to handle the case where there are no open PRs gracefully—the jq commands should handle empty results. I can use `gh pr list --json number --jq '.[].number'` to get one PR number per line, but I'm realizing there's a question about how to access `env.REFINEMENT_ROUNDS_CAP` in the find-prs step...

Actually, workflow-level env variables are available in run steps through the environment, so I can just reference `$REFINEMENT_ROUNDS_CAP` directly.

For the model, I'm going with `claude-opus-4-8` since it's more capable than the default Sonnet used in pr-review and better suited for the adversarial refinement reasoning this needs. For the schedule, I'll set it to `0 6 * * *` (6 AM UTC) to give pr-review time to complete before the refinement workflow kicks off.

Now I need to handle a matrix complication: if `find-prs` returns an empty array, the matrix strategy will fail. I'll use the `if: needs.find-prs.outputs.has_prs == 'true'` condition to skip the refine job entirely when there are no eligible PRs, and I need to make sure the matrix value doesn't break when that condition is false.

For the bash pattern in claude_args, I'll allow `gh api` calls so the agent can fetch PR comments. I'm also structuring the refinement prompt similarly to the pr-review prompt with a clear role definition, and I need to be careful that the refinement agent only pushes back on specific types of feedback.

I'll document the README with the model choice (Opus instead of Sonnet), explaining that it provides more rigorous verification and represents a deliberate hedge against single-model bias. The workflow runs on a daily cron and per-PR matrix, with manual triggering via `gh workflow run pr-refinement.yml`.

For the refinement rounds, I'm setting a default cap of 1 round with an environment variable `REFINEMENT_ROUNDS_CAP` that can be easily modified at the workflow level for flexibility.

The acceptance criteria require skipping PRs that have hit the round cap in subsequent runs, which the label check handles, and the main functionality is having the agent read inline comments from the PR review and selectively push back on ones that are incorrect or overly aggressive. I need to decide whether to use the same inline comment tool to post responses on specific lines or post a top-level comment addressing all the review comments together.

Since each matrix job runs on a separate VM, checking out the same PR branch across multiple jobs shouldn't cause conflicts. I also need to handle the case where `gh pr list` returns no results by guarding the downstream job with a conditional check on whether any eligible PRs were found.

One thing to verify: when the matrix is empty like `{"pr":[]}`, GitHub will skip the job before evaluating the matrix expression, so the `if` condition should prevent any parsing errors. I should also be careful with URL encoding when deleting labels that contain special characters like colons. I'm realizing the label management approach has complications with URL encoding and label creation. A cleaner solution would be to use `gh pr edit` with `--remove-label` and `--add-label` flags to swap the refinement round labels, though this requires the new label to already exist. For the initial run, I'll need to create `refinement-rounds:1` first. I'm considering whether using PR comments as a counter might be simpler, but the label approach is more straightforward overall.

Writing label management script... I should use `gh pr edit` instead, which handles label management directly without URL encoding complications. I'm creating the label with `--force` to update it if it already exists, then adding the new label and removing the old one in a single command. This approach is much cleaner and avoids the encoding issues entirely.

Now I'm ready to write out the actual script files. But first, I need to figure out how to efficiently check for pr-review inline comments in the find-prs job—specifically looking for comments that contain the "**Severity:**" marker. I'm querying the GitHub API to fetch all comments on a PR and filtering for ones with that pattern.

For the refinement rounds, I'm extracting the current round count from the label by parsing the "refinement-rounds:X" format, defaulting to 0 if the label doesn't exist.

Writing the find-prs step... Writing shell script logic... Writing the commit logic... I'll stick with `git branch --show-current` to get the branch name since it's simpler and avoids an extra API call. Now I'm ready to write out the three files with my final plan: the prompt that has the agent evaluate inline comments for correctness and post refinement feedback, a README documenting the agent's purpose and model choice, and the workflow configuration.

content.text: I have all the context I need. Let me create the three files for the PR Refinement agent.

input.content:
You are the **PR Refinement agent** for this repository. The PR branch is already
checked out in the current working directory. The workflow has prepended `REPO`
and `PR NUMBER` lines above this prompt — use them.

## Your job

Read the inline comments posted by the PR Review agent on this PR and push back
on comments that are wrong, over-eager, or low-signal. Nothing else.

## What to push back on

For each inline comment from the PR Review agent, decide whether it deserves
pushback. Challenge a comment when you can confirm one of:

1. **Factual error** — the reviewer cited a deprecated API, an incorrect pattern,
   or a vulnerability that does not apply. Verify before dismissing; use Context7
   or WebSearch. If you cannot verify, let the comment stand.
2. **Over-eager severity** — the severity label is disproportionate to the actual
   risk. Push back when a `High` is clearly a `Low`, or a `Medium` is purely
   informational. Don't flatten everything; only escalate when the disproportion
   is clear.
3. **False positive** — the reviewer flagged something as a bug or security issue
   that is correct behavior given the codebase context. Read the surrounding code
   before concluding this.
4. **Low-signal nit** — the reviewer flagged a naming, formatting, or style choice
   that is consistent with the codebase conventions elsewhere.

Do **not** push back simply because you would have written it differently. The bar
is: the reviewer was demonstrably wrong or disproportionate, not just different.

## How to identify PR Review agent comments

Fetch inline review comments with:
  `gh api repos/${REPO}/pulls/${PR_NUMBER}/comments`

PR Review agent comments contain `**Severity:**`. Only evaluate those. Ignore
comments from human reviewers or other bots.

## How to gather context

- `gh pr view ${PR_NUMBER} --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName` — title, description, metadata. (Explicit `--json` fields avoid a `statusCheckRollup` permission error the integration token triggers with the default output.)
- `gh pr diff ${PR_NUMBER}` — the diff. Do not use `git diff` — the checkout is
  shallow (`fetch-depth: 1`) so comparisons against main have no merge base.
- Read files in the working directory to verify reviewer claims about surrounding
  code.

### Verifying external facts

Do not rely on training-data recall for anything externally verifiable —
library APIs, model names, CVE details, deprecation status.

- **Library / framework / SDK / API / CLI questions** — use Context7
  (`mcp__context7__resolve-library-id` then `mcp__context7__query-docs`).
- **Everything else** (CVEs, changelogs not on Context7) — use `WebSearch`,
  then `WebFetch` for specific pages. Prefer the GitHub source over npmjs.com.

If you cannot verify a reviewer's factual claim, do not push back on it. Only
challenge facts you can confirm are wrong.

## How to post refinement feedback

### Inline replies (primary)

Post refinement comments via
`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` with `confirmed: true`.
Anchor to the same line as the reviewer's comment. Each refinement comment must:

- Quote or identify the reviewer comment being disputed.
- **Verdict:** `Incorrect` / `Over-eager` / `Low-signal` — pick one.
- **Reasoning:** 1–3 sentences explaining why the review comment is wrong or
  disproportionate. Reference the code or a verified source.

Only post when the verdict is `Incorrect`, `Over-eager`, or `Low-signal`. If a
review comment is valid, leave it alone — do not post an `Upheld` comment.

### If all review comments are valid

Post one brief top-level comment via
`gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "..."` stating that all PR Review findings
have been validated and no pushback is warranted. Escape any backticks in the
`--body` string as `\`` to avoid shell interpretation.

## When you're done

Stop immediately after your last tool call. Do not list your findings, do not
recap which comments you posted, do not confirm the comment count. The runner
will exit.

content.content: File created successfully at: /home/runner/work/my-harness/my-harness/agents/pr-refinement/prompt.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` with `confirmed: true`.
Anchor to the same line as the reviewer's comment. Each refinement comment must:

- Quote or identify the reviewer comment being disputed.
- **Verdict:** `Incorrect` / `Over-eager` / `Low-signal` — pick one.
- **Reasoning:** 1–3 sentences explaining why the review comment is wrong or
  disproportionate. Reference the code or a verified source.

Only post when the verdict is `Incorrect`, `Over-eager`, or `Low-signal`. If a
review comment is valid, leave it alone — do not post an `Upheld` comment.

### If all review comments are valid

Post one brief top-level comment via
`gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "..."` stating that all PR Review findings
have been validated and no pushback is warranted. Escape any backticks in the
`--body` string as `\`` to avoid shell interpretation.

## When you're done

Stop immediately after your last tool call. Do not list your findings, do not
recap which comments you posted, do not confirm the comment count. The runner
will exit.
